items.correct: A piano whose timbre has been transformed by inserting objects such as bolts and rubber between the strings.
items.misconception: A prepared piano is simply a piano that has been tuned and serviced before the concert.
items.random_wrong: A prepared piano is a digital keyboard with preset sounds.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'prepared' misleads students; Cage's preparation deliberately changes the instrument's sound into percussive, gamelan-like timbres.

items.q: Do 'atonal' and 'dissonant' mean the same thing?
items.correct: No - dissonance can occur within tonal music, while atonality means the absence of any key centre.
items.misconception: Any music with harsh dissonances must be atonal.
items.random_wrong: Atonal means music performed without a conductor.
items.why: The Rite of Spring is ferociously dissonant yet built on folk-derived pitch centres, showing that dissonance and the abandonment of tonality are separate innovations.

items.q: Does chance music mean the composer did no composing?
items.correct: No - the composer designs the framework, materials and rules; chance decides only the elements deliberately left open.
items.misconception: In chance music nothing is composed and performers do whatever they like.
items.random_wrong: Chance music is generated by rolling dice on stage instead of playing instruments.
items.why: Indeterminate works are carefully constructed systems; understanding what is fixed versus what is free is the analytical key to this repertoire.

items.q: The prepared piano is most closely associated with which composer?
items.why: It's John Cage. “Joseph Haydn” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which ballet score famously provoked uproar at its 1913 Paris premiere?
items.why: Correct answer: Stravinsky's 'The Rite of Spring'. “Tchaikovsky's 'Swan Lake'” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: 'Live electronics' in contemporary music means what?
items.correct: Electronic processing of sound during the performance itself
items.misconception: Playing a recording of synthesised music before the concert
items.random_wrong: Using electric light effects on stage
items.why: Correct answer: Electronic processing of sound during the performance itself. “Playing a recording of synthesised music before the concert” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Leaving aspects of the music to chance or performer choice
items.why: It's Leaving aspects of the music to chance or performer choice. “Writing without bar lines” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Stravinsky's 'The Rite of Spring' first performed in Paris?
items.why: Exact answer: 1913. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
